title: BUILD Grant Program (formerly RAISE)
summary: Competitive surface transportation funding for projects with significant local or regional impact, including roads, bridges, transit access, ports, and multimodal corridors.
amount_label: $1M-$25M typical federal request
match_amount: Local match is commonly expected; amount varies by project type and statutory rules.
eligibility: States and the District of Columbia
Cities, counties, and other units of local government
Transit agencies, port authorities, and transportation authorities
Federally recognized Tribal governments
Multi-jurisdictional applicant teams
how_to_apply: Confirm the project is an eligible surface transportation investment and identify the lead applicant.
Download the current BUILD NOFO and follow the required application outline and page limits.
Prepare benefit, readiness, equity, safety, and regional impact narratives with supporting maps and cost estimates.
Secure match documentation, governing body support, and any partner letters before submission.
allowable_uses: Transit access and multimodal station-area projects
Freight, port, and rail access improvements
Planning, design, and preconstruction for eligible transportation projects
key_restrictions: Project must align with BUILD statutory eligibility and evaluation criteria.
Applications need credible readiness, cost, and delivery assumptions.
Environmental review, right-of-way, and procurement risks can weaken competitiveness.
Local match and long-term operations assumptions should be documented.
documents_needed: Letters of support and match commitment
beacon_read: Best for governments with a transportation project that is already scoped, politically backed, and close to delivery.
good_fit_for: Cities with shovel-ready corridor or safety projects
Regional transportation partnerships
Counties that can document local or state match
Agencies with strong freight, equity, or safety outcomes

title: Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment (BEAD) Program
summary: A $42.45 billion broadband infrastructure program that flows through states and territories to support deployment, planning, adoption, and community anchor connectivity.
amount_label: State-administered; subgrant sizes vary by state project area
match_amount: Subgrantee match rules vary by state and NTIA guidance; many deployments require non-federal cost share.
eligibility: States, territories, and the District of Columbia receive NTIA awards
Internet service providers and public/private entities apply through state BEAD processes
Local governments can participate as applicants or partners where allowed by state rules
Tribal and community anchor partnerships are often encouraged
geography: Nationwide, administered through state and territorial programs
how_to_apply: Identify your state or territory BEAD portal and confirm whether a prequalification or challenge-process step is required.
Review the state's approved final proposal, scoring criteria, and eligible project areas.
Assemble engineering, service-area, affordability, and compliance materials required by the state.
Submit through the state program rather than directly to NTIA.
allowable_uses: Deployment or upgrade of broadband infrastructure in unserved and underserved areas
Adoption, workforce, and related eligible nondeployment activities where permitted
key_restrictions: Most applicants cannot apply directly to NTIA; awards flow through states and territories.
State challenge process outcomes determine eligible locations.
Buy America, labor, and compliance rules are significant.
Project scopes must align with the state's final proposal and NTIA rules.
beacon_read: Usually not a direct city-hall grant; local governments are strongest when they partner early with their state broadband office or a qualified provider.
good_fit_for: States and territorial broadband offices
Municipalities partnering with providers on unserved areas
Regional broadband collaboratives
Projects tied to community anchor institutions or rural service gaps

title: Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Entitlement Program
agency: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
summary: Annual formula funding for entitlement cities and urban counties to support housing, public facilities, economic development, and neighborhood revitalization activities.
amount_label: Formula allocation; varies by jurisdiction and annual HUD award
eligibility: Entitlement cities
Urban counties
Local governments operating through HUD-approved plans
Subrecipients designated by entitlement grantees
how_to_apply: Confirm the jurisdiction's entitlement status and current annual action plan cycle.
Develop a project that meets a CDBG national objective, usually low- and moderate-income benefit.
Complete local citizen participation and public notice requirements.
Submit through the local entitlement process, not as a stand-alone Grants.gov application in most cases.
allowable_uses: Public facilities and infrastructure serving low- and moderate-income residents
Housing rehabilitation and related code activities
Certain public services within statutory caps
key_restrictions: Activities must meet a HUD national objective.
Environmental review is required before choice-limiting actions.
Public services are capped.
Projects must fit within the grantee's consolidated plan and annual action plan.
beacon_read: Flexible and familiar, but only if your jurisdiction already has an entitlement allocation or a strong path through a state or local CDBG administrator.
good_fit_for: Entitlement cities with housing or facility needs
Counties serving low- and moderate-income neighborhoods
Downtown or corridor revitalization efforts
Projects that can satisfy HUD documentation rules

title: Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities (BRIC)
summary: Competitive hazard-mitigation funding for infrastructure and resilience projects that reduce long-term risk from natural hazards.
amount_label: Up to $150M maximum federal share shown in current listing
match_amount: Cost share is generally required and varies by applicant and project circumstances.
eligibility: States and territories
Federally recognized Tribal governments
Local governments applying through a state or territory
District of Columbia and eligible territorial entities
how_to_apply: Coordinate with your state hazard mitigation office before developing the final application.
Confirm the project is in an eligible mitigation plan and meets FEMA technical requirements.
Prepare benefit-cost documentation, engineering, and environmental materials.
Submit through FEMA GO and the applicable state review process before the federal deadline.
allowable_uses: Flood control, drainage, and stormwater resilience projects
Wildfire, wind, seismic, and other hazard mitigation infrastructure
Building code adoption and related resilience capacity tied to infrastructure
Utility hardening and critical facility protection
key_restrictions: Local governments usually cannot apply directly to FEMA and must go through the state.
Projects need strong technical, mitigation-plan, and benefit-cost support.
FEMA environmental and historic preservation review can be substantial.
Capability-building is narrower than many applicants expect.
beacon_read: Highly competitive nationally; strongest for communities with a clear hazard story, a ready sponsor, and match lined up before submission.
good_fit_for: Flood-prone cities with engineered mitigation projects
Counties with repeated disaster losses
Jurisdictions that already have FEMA-ready planning and technical support
Projects with community resilience co-benefits

title: Climate Pollution Reduction Grants (CPRG) Implementation Grants
summary: Large-scale climate implementation funding tied to Priority Climate Action Plans, supporting cross-sector greenhouse gas reduction measures.
amount_label: $2M-$500M based on competition type and project scale
eligibility: States and executive branch agencies
Eligible municipalities and metropolitan entities
Tribes, Tribal consortia, and territories
Coalitions implementing measures from an applicable Priority Climate Action Plan
how_to_apply: Confirm the proposed measures are included in an applicable Priority Climate Action Plan.
Develop implementation, emissions-reduction, equity, and management plans tied to the NOFO.
Prepare federal forms, budget tables, and required climate-benefit documentation.
Submit through the EPA competition identified in the NOFO.
allowable_uses: Building decarbonization and energy efficiency programs
Waste, methane, and industrial emissions reduction measures
Climate implementation staffing and program delivery tied to funded measures
key_restrictions: Projects must align to eligible planning documents and EPA competition rules.
This was a large, one-time implementation competition and is not always open.
Applications needed strong quantified emissions reduction methods.
Implementation reporting and federal grant management expectations are substantial.
documents_needed: Priority Climate Action Plan alignment materials
beacon_read: Best for agencies with an adopted climate plan and a portfolio that can show measurable emissions reductions quickly and credibly.
good_fit_for: State climate or environmental agencies
Large metro coalitions with ready implementation measures
Jurisdictions with cross-sector greenhouse gas reduction plans
Applicants that can quantify community benefits and emissions outcomes

title: Title I, Part A: Improving Basic Programs Operated by Local Educational Agencies
summary: Formula funding for states and school districts serving high-poverty schools and students, supporting academic interventions and school improvement.
amount_label: Formula allocation; varies by state and district poverty counts
eligibility: State educational agencies receive formula allocations
Local educational agencies receive suballocations from states
Eligible schools operate targeted assistance or schoolwide programs
how_to_apply: Work through your state education agency's consolidated application or annual federal programs process.
Confirm district poverty data, reservations, and school eligibility calculations.
Prepare school plans, parent engagement requirements, and budgeting documentation.
Submit on the state's timeline rather than a national Grants.gov competition.
allowable_uses: Extended learning time and summer supports
key_restrictions: This is a formula program, not a typical national competitive grant.
Supplement-not-supplant and comparability rules apply.
Funds must be targeted according to Title I requirements.
State and district planning requirements drive the local process.
beacon_read: More of a planning and compliance funding stream than a chase grant; the main opportunity is using it strategically, not winning a competition.
good_fit_for: School districts with high-poverty campuses
Districts building stronger intervention supports
LEAs that can align federal funds across school improvement needs

title: NEA Grants for Arts Projects
summary: Project-based support for arts engagement, arts education, community-centered arts programming, and organizational capacity in the arts sector.
amount_label: $10K-$100K in the most recent cycle
eligibility: Nonprofit 501(c)(3) organizations
Units of state or local government
Federally recognized Tribal communities or tribes
Some higher education institutions and school districts
how_to_apply: Review the discipline-specific application instructions and project type guidance.
Register organizational identifiers and complete any required federal system registrations in advance.
Prepare project narrative, budget, work samples, and community engagement details.
Submit the required federal forms and the NEA application materials by the cycle deadline.
allowable_uses: Community arts engagement and cultural access projects
Capacity-building activities tied to the project
key_restrictions: Requires a 1:1 match.
Individuals and fiscal sponsors are generally not eligible.
Projects must align with NEA project categories and applicant rules.
The application is competitive and review criteria are program-specific.
documents_needed: Work samples or artistic support materials
beacon_read: Most competitive when the arts project is concrete, community-facing, and paired with a believable match and delivery team.
good_fit_for: Municipal arts agencies
Museums and cultural nonprofits
School districts or universities with public arts programming
Projects serving rural or underserved communities

title: Transit Investments for Greenhouse Gas and Energy Reduction (TIGGER)
summary: Legacy FTA sustainability funding for transit capital investments that reduce energy use and greenhouse gas emissions.
amount_label: $1M-$15M in the FY 2011 solicitation
match_amount: Local cost share applied under FTA rules.
eligibility: Public transportation agencies
State DOTs
Federally recognized Tribes
Eligible transit consortia
how_to_apply: Review the legacy TIGGER solicitation and confirm whether the project better fits a current FTA clean-transit program.
Develop a capital project with measurable greenhouse gas or energy-reduction benefits.
Prepare transit capital budget, technical scope, and local cost-share materials.
Use this entry mainly as historical reference when benchmarking modern transit decarbonization grants.
allowable_uses: Facility upgrades tied to transit sustainability outcomes
key_restrictions: This is a legacy program and is no longer an active funding round.
Current applicants should look at Low-No, Bus Facilities, or other active FTA programs instead.
The original program was limited to capital investments with measurable outcomes.
beacon_read: Useful as a precedent signal for clean-transit investments, but not a live opportunity; modern FTA clean-fleet programs are the practical path now.
good_fit_for: Transit agencies studying legacy federal clean-transit models
Teams benchmarking future zero- or low-emission bus proposals

title: EPA Brownfields Assessment Grants
summary: Funding for inventories, site assessments, cleanup planning, and community engagement related to brownfield revitalization.
amount_label: $500K-$2M depending on grant type and applicant category
eligibility: States and Tribes
Local governments and quasi-governmental entities
Regional councils and redevelopment authorities
Certain nonprofits where allowed by EPA guidance
how_to_apply: Choose the appropriate Brownfields grant type and verify eligibility for that track.
Prepare a site inventory, community need case, and revolving or redevelopment strategy as applicable.
Develop threshold, ranking, and budget responses using EPA guidance.
Submit the full package through Grants.gov by the EPA deadline.
allowable_uses: Community-wide brownfield inventories and site assessments
key_restrictions: Assessment grants do not fund cleanup itself.
EPA has detailed threshold criteria and site eligibility rules.
Applicants need a clear redevelopment or community-benefit strategy.
Cleanup and revolving loan fund tracks have different ownership and leverage expectations.
beacon_read: A strong fit when a city knows which sites are holding back redevelopment but still needs environmental due diligence to unlock them.
good_fit_for: Cities with former industrial or commercial sites
Redevelopment authorities
Communities building a downtown or corridor reuse pipeline
Applicants with visible environmental justice needs

title: HOME Investment Partnerships Program
agency: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
summary: Formula grants to states and participating jurisdictions for affordable housing development, rehabilitation, homeownership assistance, and tenant-based rental assistance.
amount_label: Formula allocation; varies by participating jurisdiction
match_amount: Participating jurisdictions generally must provide a 25% match, subject to HUD rules and waivers.
eligibility: States
Participating jurisdictions designated by HUD
Local governments and housing partners acting through a participating jurisdiction
Community housing development organizations through PJ allocations
how_to_apply: Confirm whether your city or county is a HOME participating jurisdiction or must apply through the state.
Align the project to the consolidated plan, annual action plan, and HOME underwriting rules.
Complete subsidy layering, environmental review, and affordability planning.
Submit through the local or state HOME allocation process rather than a national competition.
allowable_uses: New construction or rehabilitation of affordable housing
Acquisition and development costs tied to eligible housing
key_restrictions: This is a formula program and local access depends on HUD participation status.
Affordability periods, underwriting, and HOME property standards apply.
Projects need environmental clearance and subsidy review.
Match, income targeting, and written agreement rules are significant.
documents_needed: HOME underwriting and subsidy review materials
beacon_read: A practical housing capital source when your jurisdiction already controls HOME dollars or has a strong state relationship and a finance-ready project.
good_fit_for: Cities with affordable rental development pipelines
Counties supporting homeownership or rehab programs
Projects that can handle long-term compliance and affordability rules

title: ReConnect Loan and Grant Program
summary: Broadband deployment funding for rural areas through grants, loans, and loan-grant combinations for last-mile infrastructure projects.
amount_label: Up to $50M depending on product type
match_amount: Varies by product; some grant products require a 25% match while certain categories may have no match.
eligibility: States and local governments
Tribal governments
Corporations, LLCs, cooperatives, and mutual organizations
Territories and other eligible rural broadband entities
how_to_apply: Use USDA mapping tools to confirm the proposed service area is eligible.
Choose the correct product type: grant, loan, or loan-grant combination.
Prepare engineering, financial, and service-area materials required by USDA.
Submit through the USDA ReConnect application system before the round deadline.
allowable_uses: Construction or improvement of broadband facilities
Acquisition of equipment needed to deliver eligible broadband service
Associated deployment costs tied to the funded network
key_restrictions: Service-area eligibility is highly map-dependent.
Technical and financial capacity reviews are substantial.
Different product types have different match and underwriting expectations.
This is geared toward broadband deployment, not general digital equity programming.
beacon_read: Strongest for rural broadband builds with an experienced operator, a defensible service area, and the patience for a very technical application.
good_fit_for: Rural counties partnering with broadband providers
Public utility or cooperative broadband projects
Tribal governments with underserved service areas
Local governments filling true last-mile gaps

title: Grid Resilience and Innovation Partnerships (GRIP) / SPARK Round 3
summary: Large-scale grid modernization funding focused in the current round on accelerated reconductoring and advanced transmission upgrades under the SPARK NOFO.
amount_label: Current listing shows a $250M federal share floor; project sizes vary
match_amount: Significant non-federal cost share is required under DOE program rules.
eligibility: States and Tribal governments
Electric utilities and grid operators
Other eligible electric-sector entities identified in the NOFO
Partnerships able to carry large infrastructure projects
how_to_apply: Review the SPARK / GRIP Round 3 NOFO and verify the project fits the round's technology and delivery emphasis.
Prepare concept paper and application materials on DOE's schedule.
Develop technical, cost-share, labor, and permitting strategies suitable for major grid infrastructure work.
Submit through DOE's required application systems and formats.
allowable_uses: Resilience and modernization investments tied to eligible grid infrastructure
Associated project delivery and implementation costs allowed by DOE
key_restrictions: This is a very large-infrastructure competition with major cost-share expectations.
Projects need mature utility partners and technical readiness.
Most local governments are better positioned as partners than sole leads.
Federal labor, domestic sourcing, and infrastructure compliance requirements are significant.
documents_needed: Concept paper and full application narrative
beacon_read: Usually not a city-hall solo play; it makes the most sense when a government is part of a utility-led transmission or resilience partnership with real scale.
good_fit_for: State energy offices working with utilities
Large regional grid infrastructure partnerships
Projects with clear reconductoring or transmission-upgrade scope
Applicants able to manage heavy federal infrastructure compliance

title: Emergency Management Performance Grant (EMPG)
summary: Annual emergency management funding that supports all-hazards preparedness, planning, staffing, training, exercises, and core capability development.
amount_label: Formula allocation through states; local pass-through amounts vary
match_amount: EMPG generally requires a 50% non-federal cost share.
eligibility: States and territories apply directly to FEMA
Local emergency management agencies typically receive pass-through funding from the state
Tribal and territorial entities as allowed under program rules
how_to_apply: Confirm your state emergency management agency's local application process and timeline.
Align the request to the state's EMPG work plan and preparedness priorities.
Prepare staffing, planning, training, exercise, or technology justifications tied to core capabilities.
Document the local cost share and any regional coordination expectations.
allowable_uses: Equipment and technology tied to preparedness needs
Programs that build and sustain core capabilities
key_restrictions: Local agencies usually access funds through the state, not directly from FEMA.
A 50% cost share is a major practical constraint.
Work plans must align with FEMA and state preparedness guidance.
Allowable costs are broad but still bounded by preparedness grant rules.
documents_needed: State or local EMPG application forms
beacon_read: More dependable than flashy, but very useful when a city or county wants to fund core emergency management capacity instead of one-off equipment asks.
good_fit_for: County emergency management offices
Regional preparedness collaborations
Jurisdictions sustaining planning and exercise capacity
Agencies that can cover the local match

title: Maximizing Investigators' Research Award (MIRA)
agency: National Institute of General Medical Sciences / NIH
summary: Investigator-focused biomedical research support that gives eligible NIGMS investigators flexible, stable funding for an integrated research program.
amount_label: Typically starts around $250K/year in direct costs; total award varies
eligibility: Eligible NIGMS-funded investigators
New investigators proposing research within the NIGMS mission
Domestic applicant organizations meeting NIH eligibility rules
how_to_apply: Review the MIRA NOFO and confirm the investigator and institution are eligible for that specific track.
Develop a program-level scientific narrative rather than a narrow project scope.
Prepare NIH biosketches, facilities, budget, and research strategy materials.
Submit through NIH application systems by the relevant due date.
allowable_uses: Fundamental biomedical research within the NIGMS mission
Flexible support across an investigator's broader research program
Research personnel, supplies, and related allowable NIH costs
key_restrictions: This is aimed at research institutions, not local government applicants.
Eligibility differs for established and early-stage investigator tracks.
Foreign subaward restrictions have changed under NIH policy.
Scientific fit to NIGMS mission is central.
beacon_read: Not a government-agency fit, but important for Beacon's university audience because it rewards strong investigator programs rather than one tightly scoped project.
good_fit_for: Research universities
Academic medical or biomedical research institutions
Investigators building a long-term NIGMS-aligned lab program

title: Faculty Early Career Development Program (CAREER)
cfda: Multiple NSF assistance listings by directorate
summary: NSF's flagship early-career faculty competition supporting integrated research and education plans over five years.
amount_label: At least $400K total over 5 years; some directorates support more
eligibility: Early-career faculty at CAREER-eligible institutions
Certain nonprofit, non-academic research and education organizations
Principal investigators meeting NSF CAREER eligibility rules
how_to_apply: Confirm the PI meets NSF's CAREER eligibility rules and has institutional endorsement.
Build a proposal that tightly integrates the research agenda with an education plan.
Follow the NSF solicitation and any directorate-specific guidance.
Submit through Research.gov or Grants.gov before the annual deadline.
allowable_uses: Student and trainee support consistent with NSF rules
Project dissemination and broader impacts activities
key_restrictions: Only one CAREER proposal may be submitted per annual competition by a PI.
The project must integrate research and education in a credible way.
Institutional endorsement and fit to NSF program priorities matter.
This is a university-focused opportunity, not a city grant.
beacon_read: A core university opportunity, but not a government one; the differentiator is a faculty member with a clear five-year research and education vision.
good_fit_for: Tenure-track faculty at eligible institutions
Universities building strong junior-faculty grant pipelines
Applicants with aligned research and teaching strategies

title: Preventive Health and Health Services (PHHS) Block Grant
agency: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
summary: Flexible preventive-health funding for states, tribes, territories, and freely associated states to address locally defined public-health priorities.
amount_label: Formula/block grant allocation; varies by recipient
eligibility: All 50 states
District of Columbia
Certain tribes, territories, and freely associated states
how_to_apply: Confirm recipient eligibility and the annual CDC block grant guidance.
Set local public-health priorities and select evidence-based or locally justified activities.
Prepare work plan, budget, and performance reporting materials.
Submit through CDC's required grant process and annual timeline.
allowable_uses: Public-health activities addressing locally prioritized needs
key_restrictions: This is a block grant for eligible public-health recipients, not a broad local competition.
Use is flexible but still tied to block-grant guidance and reporting.
Local governments usually access impact through state or tribal recipients.
Funds should address public-health priorities not fully covered elsewhere.
beacon_read: Flexible money with real value, but usually not directly available to city departments unless they are working closely with the state health agency or an eligible tribal recipient.
good_fit_for: State public-health agencies
Tribal public-health entities
Jurisdictions addressing emerging or underfunded prevention needs

title: WIOA Youth Formula Program
summary: Formula workforce funding for youth employment, education, and supportive services delivered through state and local workforce systems.
amount_label: Formula allocation through states and local workforce areas
eligibility: States receive allotments under WIOA
Local workforce development areas administer funds
Workforce boards and service providers deliver services
how_to_apply: Determine whether your organization is a local workforce board, subrecipient, or service partner.
Review the state's local planning and procurement process for WIOA youth services.
Design services around out-of-school and in-school youth requirements.
Apply through the state or local workforce system rather than a single national competition.
key_restrictions: This is a formula system, not a standard open Grants.gov competition for most local entities.
Local boards and states control procurement and program design.
WIOA youth spending requirements, participant eligibility, and performance rules are detailed.
Programs must fit local and state workforce plans.
beacon_read: Best treated as a system-partnership funding stream: the strongest angle is aligning with a local workforce board instead of pursuing it like a stand-alone grant.
good_fit_for: Local workforce boards
County or regional youth workforce partnerships
Nonprofits contracted by workforce systems
Programs focused on disconnected youth or career pathways

title: Community Services Block Grant (CSBG)
summary: Flexible anti-poverty funding that supports states, tribes, territories, and local eligible entities delivering community-based services and self-sufficiency strategies.
amount_label: Formula allocation through states and eligible entities
eligibility: States, tribes, and territories receive allocations
Local eligible entities such as Community Action Agencies receive pass-through support
Community-based service providers partner with local CSBG recipients
how_to_apply: Identify the state CSBG office or local eligible entity serving your area.
Review the community action plan or local anti-poverty strategy already in place.
Develop services or partnerships that match local CSBG priorities and performance goals.
Apply through the state or local CSBG process rather than a national open competition.
allowable_uses: Transportation, nutrition, and family stabilization supports
key_restrictions: Most organizations access CSBG through state or local intermediaries.
Uses are flexible but tied to anti-poverty outcomes and accountability requirements.
Local community action systems usually shape what is fundable in practice.
beacon_read: Not a splashy capital source, but very valuable where a local government wants to partner with existing anti-poverty infrastructure instead of building a new program from scratch.
good_fit_for: Community Action Agencies
Local governments partnering on poverty-reduction services
Regional human-services collaboratives
Programs focused on housing stability, employment, or crisis response

title: Build to Scale
summary: EDA funding for regional innovation and entrepreneurship systems, including accelerators, capital access models, and technology commercialization support.
amount_label: $1-$5M federal request range shown in the FY 2024 listing
match_amount: Non-federal share is required under EDA rules.
eligibility: State and local governments
Tribal governments
Higher education institutions
Nonprofits and innovation-support organizations
how_to_apply: Select the Build to Scale track that best matches your innovation or capital-access strategy.
Demonstrate the regional innovation need, target sector, and operational model.
Prepare budget, match, and organizational capacity materials.
Submit through EDA's required application process with all optional and required attachments that strengthen competitiveness.
key_restrictions: Funds generally support ecosystem operations and programming, not direct startup grants to participants.
Regional economic logic and innovation focus are central.
Applicants need a credible post-award operating model and match.
beacon_read: Best for regional innovation intermediaries, not routine local-government service delivery; the strongest applications show an ecosystem gap and a clear operating model.
good_fit_for: University-led innovation partnerships
Regional entrepreneurship and commercialization hubs
Economic development organizations building capital pathways
Public-private startup ecosystem collaboratives

title: Museums for America
agency: Institute of Museum and Library Services
summary: Project-based support for museums advancing learning, community engagement, and collections stewardship through strategic public-facing work.
amount_label: $5K-$300K in the FY 2026 cycle
match_amount: Cost share is required under Museums for America rules.
eligibility: Museums meeting IMLS institutional eligibility requirements
Nonprofit museums and certain public museum entities
Applicants aligned to Museums for America project categories
how_to_apply: Choose the right Museums for America project category and confirm institutional eligibility.
Develop a clear public-service outcome, work plan, and budget.
Prepare narrative, digital forms, and required attachments through the IMLS process.
Submit by the annual competition deadline.
allowable_uses: Digital or operational projects tied to museum public service
key_restrictions: Applicants must meet IMLS museum eligibility standards.
Projects should be strategic and outcome-driven rather than generic operating support.
Cost share applies.
The strongest proposals tie museum work to public impact.
beacon_read: A strong fit when a museum can clearly connect the project to public value, not just internal operations.
good_fit_for: Municipal or county museums
History, science, and cultural museums
Museums expanding learning and community engagement
Collections access and stewardship projects with public benefit
